content.text: Action Item Ownership and SLAs

A postmortem that produces action items without enforcing their completion is a postmortem culture in name only. The most common failure mode for postmortem programs is not poor quality postmortems — it is good quality postmortems whose action items are opened, assigned, and then silently deprioritized as the incident fades from memory. Action item ownership and SLAs are the operational mechanisms that prevent this.

Three principles govern effective action item management: single ownership (every item has one named engineer responsible for closure, not a team), bounded SLAs (priority determines a concrete due date, not a vague commitment), and tracked compliance (SLA adherence is measured and reported, with automatic escalation on breach). Without all three, action items accumulate in backlogs and the reliability of the system does not improve.

attrs.alt: Action item priority and SLA matrix with owner, due date, review cadence, and escalation paths

content.text: Priority Tiers and SLA Definitions

P0 action items address the conditions that allowed the incident to happen or that directly prevented detection. They must enter the active sprint immediately — not the next planning cycle — and close within the sprint. The Incident Commander is responsible for creating P0 tickets before the postmortem meeting ends, with assignees and sprint assignment confirmed in the meeting.

P1 action items address contributing factors that did not directly cause the incident but meaningfully increase risk. They enter the next sprint's backlog within 48 hours of the postmortem meeting and must close within that sprint. P2 action items are improvements that reduce systemic risk without urgency — they belong in the quarterly backlog and are tracked in monthly incident review sessions.

Deferral is a legitimate status for action items when circumstances change. An action item can be deferred if new information shows that the risk was lower than initially assessed, or if a higher-priority incident has consumed the team's capacity. The deferral must be documented with a reason and a new due date. Repeated deferral without closure is an escalation signal.

# action_item_tracker.py - track postmortem action items with SLA enforcement
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum
from typing import List, Optional
import json


class Priority(str, Enum):
    P0 = 'P0'  # current sprint
    P1 = 'P1'  # next sprint
    P2 = 'P2'  # this quarter


class Status(str, Enum):
    OPEN = 'open'
    IN_PROGRESS = 'in_progress'
    CLOSED = 'closed'
    DEFERRED = 'deferred'


@dataclass
class ActionItem:
    id: str
    incident_id: str
    description: str
    owner: str           # named individual, not a team
    priority: Priority
    due_date: date
    status: Status = Status.OPEN
    jira_ticket: Optional[str] = None
    closed_date: Optional[date] = None
    deferral_reason: Optional[str] = None

    @property
    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        if self.status == Status.CLOSED:
            return False
        return date.today() > self.due_date

    @property
    def days_overdue(self) -> int:
        if not self.is_overdue:
            return 0
        return (date.today() - self.due_date).days

    def close(self, closed_date: Optional[date] = None) -> None:
        self.status = Status.CLOSED
        self.closed_date = closed_date or date.today()


class ActionItemRegistry:
    def __init__(self):
        self.items: List[ActionItem] = []

    def add(self, item: ActionItem) -> 'ActionItemRegistry':
        self.items.append(item)
        return self

    def overdue_p0(self) -> List[ActionItem]:
        return [
            i for i in self.items
            if i.priority == Priority.P0 and i.is_overdue
        ]

    def open_by_priority(self, priority: Priority) -> List[ActionItem]:
        return [i for i in self.items if i.priority == priority and i.status != Status.CLOSED]

    def sla_compliance_report(self) -> dict:
        closed = [i for i in self.items if i.status == Status.CLOSED]
        on_time = [
            i for i in closed
            if i.closed_date and i.closed_date <= i.due_date
        ]
        return {
            'total': len(self.items),
            'closed': len(closed),
            'on_time': len(on_time),
            'sla_rate_pct': round(len(on_time) / max(1, len(closed)) * 100, 1),
            'overdue_p0_count': len(self.overdue_p0()),
            'overdue_p0_ids': [i.id for i in self.overdue_p0()],
        }

    def escalation_alerts(self) -> List[str]:
        alerts = []
        for item in self.overdue_p0():
            alerts.append(
                f'ESCALATION: {item.id} ({item.incident_id}) P0 overdue '
                f'{item.days_overdue}d. Owner: {item.owner}. '
                f'Notify: EM + VP Eng.'
            )
        return alerts


if __name__ == '__main__':
    registry = ActionItemRegistry()
    today = date.today()
    registry.add(ActionItem(
        id='AI-01', incident_id='INC-2026-0042',
        description='Add max_tool_calls=50 guardrail to agent framework',
        owner='alice', priority=Priority.P0,
        due_date=today - timedelta(days=3),  # overdue
        jira_ticket='ENG-5501',
    ))
    registry.add(ActionItem(
        id='AI-02', incident_id='INC-2026-0042',
        description='Add recursive tool call eval test to CI',
        owner='bob', priority=Priority.P0,
        due_date=today + timedelta(days=4),
        jira_ticket='ENG-5502',
        status=Status.IN_PROGRESS,
    ))
    registry.add(ActionItem(
        id='AI-03', incident_id='INC-2026-0042',
        description='Update prompt review checklist for stop conditions',
        owner='carol', priority=Priority.P1,
        due_date=today + timedelta(days=14),
        jira_ticket='ENG-5503',
    ))
    report = registry.sla_compliance_report()
    print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
    for alert in registry.escalation_alerts():
        print(alert)
Single Ownership: Why Teams Cannot Own Action Items

Assigning an action item to a team rather than an individual is the most common way to ensure it never closes. When an item is owned by 'the SRE team' or 'the platform team', no one is personally accountable for its completion. Each team member assumes another will handle it, and it drifts until the next incident exposes the same gap.

Single ownership does not mean that one person does all the work. The owner is responsible for driving the item to closure — which may involve coordinating with other engineers, unblocking dependencies, or escalating when they cannot make progress. The owner is accountable for the outcome, not necessarily for every line of code in the implementation.
Integrating Action Items into Sprint Planning

P0 and P1 action items must be treated as non-negotiable sprint commitments, not as aspirational additions. This requires the engineering manager to protect capacity for action item completion in the same way that they protect capacity for planned feature work. A common rule is to reserve 10-15% of sprint capacity for reliability work, which includes postmortem action items.

Sprint planning sessions should explicitly review open action items from recent postmortems at the start of capacity allocation. This makes action items visible alongside feature work and prevents them from being squeezed out by product pressure. When product roadmap pressure is so high that P0 action items cannot be closed within the sprint, that is an escalation point that requires engineering manager and VP Engineering awareness — not a silent deferral.

## Sprint Planning: Postmortem Action Item Review (template)

### Open P0 Items (must close this sprint)

| ID     | Description                                  | Owner   | Jira    | Due        |
|--------|----------------------------------------------|---------|---------|------------|
| AI-01  | Add max_tool_calls guardrail to agent SDK    | @alice  | ENG-5501| 2026-07-18 |
| AI-02  | Add recursive tool call eval test to CI      | @bob    | ENG-5502| 2026-07-18 |

### Open P1 Items (must close next sprint)

| ID     | Description                                  | Owner   | Jira    | Due        |
|--------|----------------------------------------------|---------|---------|------------|
| AI-03  | Update prompt review checklist               | @carol  | ENG-5503| 2026-07-31 |
| AI-04  | Add storage quota alert at 70% threshold     | @dave   | ENG-5504| 2026-07-31 |

### Capacity Allocation Rule

- Reserve 10% of sprint points for postmortem action items before scheduling features.
- If P0 items cannot fit, escalate to EM before sprint starts - do not silently defer.
- P0 items not closed by sprint end trigger an automatic EM + VP Eng escalation on Monday.

### Deferred Items (document reason + new date)

| ID     | Original Due | New Due    | Reason for Deferral              |
|--------|-------------|------------|----------------------------------|
| AI-05  | 2026-07-11  | 2026-07-25 | P0 INC-0044 consumed owner capacity |
SLA Compliance Reporting and Escalation

SLA compliance for action items should be reported monthly alongside incident metrics. The key metric is the P0 sprint close rate: the percentage of P0 action items that close within the sprint they were assigned to. A healthy program maintains a P0 sprint close rate above 90%. A rate below 70% indicates a systemic capacity or prioritization problem that requires management attention.

Automatic escalation on P0 SLA breach is a cultural enforcement mechanism. When the system notifies the engineering manager and VP Engineering every time a P0 item misses its SLA, it creates accountability pressure that manual review cannot replicate. Escalations should be informational, not punitive — the goal is to surface blockers so that management can clear them, not to blame the owner.
Tooling Integration: GitHub Issues and Slack Alerts

Manual tracking of action items in a spreadsheet or postmortem document is insufficient at scale. Effective action item management requires integration with the engineering team's existing workflow tools. Creating GitHub issues for each action item connects them to the sprint planning process, enables assignment and status tracking through pull requests, and surfaces them in code review context when the implementation is being reviewed.

Slack alerts for SLA breaches close the feedback loop in real time. Rather than waiting for the monthly review to discover that P0 items are overdue, automatic Slack notifications sent to the engineering manager and team channel on the morning of a SLA breach give the team an opportunity to unblock and close the item before it becomes a multi-week overdue problem.

# action_item_webhook.py - GitHub integration for postmortem action item tracking
# Creates GitHub issues for action items, polls for closure, reports to Slack
import os
import json
from datetime import date, datetime
from typing import Optional
import urllib.request
import urllib.parse


GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ['GITHUB_TOKEN']
GITHUB_REPO = os.environ.get('GITHUB_REPO', 'myorg/myrepo')
SLACK_WEBHOOK = os.environ.get('SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL', '')
BASE = 'https://api.github.com'


def github_request(method: str, path: str, body: Optional[dict] = None) -> dict:
    url = f'{BASE}{path}'
    data = json.dumps(body).encode() if body else None
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        url, data=data, method=method,
        headers={
            'Authorization': f'token {GITHUB_TOKEN}',
            'Accept': 'application/vnd.github+json',
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
        }
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
        return json.load(resp)


def create_action_item_issue(
    incident_id: str,
    item_id: str,
    description: str,
    owner: str,
    priority: str,
    due_date: date,
) -> str:
    label_map = {'P0': 'priority:p0', 'P1': 'priority:p1', 'P2': 'priority:p2'}
    issue = github_request(
        'POST',
        f'/repos/{GITHUB_REPO}/issues',
        {
            'title': f'[{incident_id}][{item_id}] {description}',
            'body': (
                f'**Postmortem Action Item**\n\n'
                f'- Incident: {incident_id}\n'
                f'- Item ID: {item_id}\n'
                f'- Priority: {priority}\n'
                f'- Due: {due_date}\n'
                f'- Owner: @{owner}\n\n'
                '## Description\n\n'
                f'{description}\n\n'
                '## Acceptance Criteria\n\n'
                '- [ ] Implementation complete and merged\n'
                '- [ ] Tests added (eval or unit test)\n'
                '- [ ] Postmortem updated with closure notes\n'
            ),
            'assignees': [owner],
            'labels': ['postmortem-action', label_map.get(priority, 'priority:p2')],
        }
    )
    return issue['html_url']


def notify_overdue_p0(incident_id: str, item_id: str, owner: str, days_overdue: int) -> None:
    if not SLACK_WEBHOOK:
        print(f'ESCALATION: {item_id} P0 overdue {days_overdue}d. Owner: {owner}')
        return
    payload = {
        'text': (
            f':rotating_light: *P0 Action Item SLA Breach*\n'
            f'Incident: `{incident_id}` | Item: `{item_id}`\n'
            f'Owner: @{owner} | Overdue: {days_overdue} days\n'
            f'Escalating to: Engineering Manager + VP Engineering'
        )
    }
    data = json.dumps(payload).encode()
    req = urllib.request.Request(SLACK_WEBHOOK, data=data,
                                  headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'})
    urllib.request.urlopen(req)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Demo: create issue stub (requires GITHUB_TOKEN env var)
    print('GitHub action item issue creation module.')
    print('Set GITHUB_TOKEN, GITHUB_REPO, SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL and call create_action_item_issue()')
Action Item Quality Criteria

The most common reason action items fail to close is that they are poorly specified. 'Improve monitoring' or 'review the alert configuration' are not actionable items — they are categories of work. A well-specified action item has a concrete deliverable that is unambiguous when done: 'Add an alert that fires when per-session tool call count exceeds 50 within a 5-minute window, with a runbook link in the alert body, merged to main and deployed to production by 2026-07-18.'

Action items should also specify acceptance criteria: what tests or verification steps confirm the item is complete? For agentic AI action items, acceptance criteria frequently include: 'eval regression test added to CI that reproduces the failure mode', 'prompt change validated against the full eval suite without regression', or 'new guardrail verified to fire in staging under the incident reproduction scenario.' Without acceptance criteria, 'done' is ambiguous and items close prematurely.
Key Takeaways
P0 items (current sprint), P1 items (next sprint), and P2 items (this quarter) each have defined SLAs and escalation paths for breach.
Single named ownership is mandatory: team-owned action items reliably fail to close because no individual is personally accountable.
Sprint planning must begin with a review of open action items, with 10-15% of sprint capacity reserved for reliability work before feature allocation.
The P0 sprint close rate is the primary culture health metric; below 70% indicates systemic capacity or prioritization problems requiring management intervention.
Automatic escalation to EM and VP Engineering on P0 SLA breach creates accountability pressure that manual review cannot replicate.
